message.content:
Test file: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/game-of-life.ts
Passing tests: 4
Recent Green phase: Added neighbor-counting and filter by 2-or-3 neighbors for survival (Rules 1-3 for live cells). Helpers `isAlive` and `countNeighbors` defined inside `nextGeneration`.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness ‚Äî fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly ‚Äî if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high‚Üímax: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only ‚Äî it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 ‚Üí 4.6, 4.6 ‚Üí 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.description: Run ESLint to measure smells and cognitive complexity

content.text:
**PRE measurements (ESLint):**
- Smells total: **2** (both `no-magic-numbers` for the values 2 and 3 in survival check)
- Cognitive complexity violations: 0

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

Functions in implementation:
- `nextGeneration(cells)`: Computes next generation. Name clearly reveals intent.
- `isAlive(x, y)`: Checks if a cell is alive. Name clearly reveals intent.
- `countNeighbors(x, y)`: Counts live neighbors of a cell. Name clearly reveals intent.

**Decision**: Keep all current names. They reveal intent.

## Step 2: Pre-Measurement (APP Mass)

**`nextGeneration` (outer)**:
- Constants: 2 (literals `2`, `3`)
- Bindings: 6 (cells, x, y, n, plus destructured x,y in filter callback)
- Invocations: 3 (countNeighbors, filter, ===√ó2 + ||)... let me be precise:
  - filter (1), countNeighbors (1), === (2), || (1) = 5 invocations
- Conditionals: 0 (just boolean expression, no `if`)
- Loops: 0 (filter is invocation, counted above)
- Assignments: 0

Mass: 2¬∑1 + 6¬∑1 + 5¬∑2 + 0¬∑4 + 0¬∑5 + 0¬∑6 = **18**

**`isAlive`**:
- Constants: 0
- Bindings: 4 (x, y, cx, cy)
- Invocations: 3 (some, ===, ===, &&) = 4
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0

Mass: 0 + 4 + 4¬∑2 = **12**

**`countNeighbors`**:
- Constants: 4 (literals `0`, `-1`, `1`, `0`, `0`, `0`) ‚Üí 0, -1, 1, 0, 0, 0 ‚Üí let me count: `0` (count init), `-1`, `1`, `-1`, `1`, `0`, `0` = 7 constants
- Bindings: 4 (x, y, dx, dy)
- Invocations: 3 (isAlive, +, +, ‚â§, ‚â§, ===, ===, &&) ‚Äî let's count operations: `<=` (2), `++` for dx/dy (2 invocations? these are assignments)
- Conditionals: 2 (two `if`)
- Loops: 2 (two `for`)
- Assignments: 3 (count = 0, count++, dx++ dy++) = `count = 0` (1), `count++` (1), `dx++` (1), `dy++` (1) = 4

Mass roughly: 7 + 4 + 3¬∑2 + 2¬∑4 + 2¬∑5 + 4¬∑6 = 7+4+6+8+10+24 = **59**

## Step 3: McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity

**`nextGeneration` body** (outer function):
- Start: 1
- `||` in `n === 2 || n === 3`: +1

McCabe: **2**

**`isAlive`**:
- Start: 1
- `&&` in `cx === x && cy === y`: +1

McCabe: **2**

**`countNeighbors`**:
- Start: 1
- `for` (dx): +1
- `for` (dy): +1
- `if (dx === 0 && dy === 0)`: +1 for if, +1 for &&
- `if (isAlive(...))`: +1

McCabe: **6**

**`countNeighbors` is the worst-McCabe function.** Primary target.

## Step 4: Apply ONE Refactoring

The most impactful change: the magic numbers smell (2 violations) is what ESLint actually flags. The survival predicate `n === 2 || n === 3` is the smell. Reducing magic numbers is a Rule-2 (reveal intent) win and removes both smells at once.

Let me extract a named predicate `survives` that captures intent.

content.text:
All 4 tests still pass. Now let me re-measure.

## Step 5: Post-Measurement (ESLint Smells + Cognitive)

The smell count got worse (2 ‚Üí 4). The const declaration introduced extra magic numbers AND the TS cast `as 2 | 3` introduced two more. Also, the type cast makes the code less clear. Let me revert and try a different angle.

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_17-11-43_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context ‚Äî no need to Read it back)

content.text:
Reverted. Now let me try a different angle. The `no-magic-numbers` rule is going to flag any literal use. But the rule from the spec (Game of Life Rules 2 and 3) is fundamentally about "a live cell with 2 or 3 neighbors survives". We can't truly eliminate those values ‚Äî they ARE the rule. However, looking at the code more carefully, perhaps I should focus on the worst-McCabe function (`countNeighbors`) instead.

Let me try refactoring `countNeighbors` ‚Äî the nested loops with the skip-self condition can become cleaner using a precomputed offsets array. This would reduce McCabe complexity.

content.text: Tests still pass. Now let me measure.

**POST measurements:**
- Smells total: **2** (same ‚Äî only the inherent rule constants 2, 3, which are part of the Game of Life spec)
- The literal `-1`, `0`, `1` offsets in the NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS table are NOT flagged because ESLint's `no-magic-numbers` has `ignoreArrayIndexes` / `ignore` defaults for those.

## Step 6: Post-Measurement (APP + McCabe)

**`countNeighbors` POST**:
- Constants: 0 (in body ‚Äî the offsets are in the module-level constant)
- Bindings: 4 (x, y, dx, dy)
- Invocations: 3 (filter, isAlive, +, +, `.length`) ‚Äî filter (1), isAlive (1), `+` (2), `.length` property access not really an invocation = ~4
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 0 (filter is invocation)
- Assignments: 0

Mass: 0 + 4 + 4¬∑2 = **12** (down from ~59)

**`countNeighbors` McCabe POST**:
- Start: 1
- No branches (filter callback has no `&&`/`||`/`if`)

McCabe: **1** (down from 6!)

**`nextGeneration` McCabe** (outer): still 2 (one `||`).

## Step 7: Report

**Refactoring Applied**: Replaced nested `for` loops with skip-self condition by a precomputed `NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS` constant + `filter`.

**Pre/Post Deltas (function `countNeighbors`)**:
- ESLint smells (whole file): 2 ‚Üí 2 (no change ‚Äî remaining smells are the Game of Life survival constants 2/3, which encode the spec rules themselves)
- APP mass: ~59 ‚Üí ~12
- McCabe cyclomatic: 6 ‚Üí 1

**Tests**: 4/4 passing (6 todo).

**Benefits**:
- `countNeighbors` body is one line of intent: "of the 8 neighbor offsets, count the ones that are alive"
- No mutable accumulator, no nested loops, no skip-self special case
- The Moore neighborhood is now a named, reusable constant rather than implicit in two loop bounds + a guard
- Naming retained ‚Äî `isAlive`, `countNeighbors`, `nextGeneration` all still reveal intent

**Files changed**:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_17-11-43_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts`

**Reverted attempt**: First tried extracting `SURVIVAL_NEIGHBOR_COUNTS = [2, 3]` and a `survives` helper. ESLint smell count went from 2 to 4 (the constant declaration itself contained the magic numbers, plus a TS `as 2 | 3` cast added two more). Reverted and chose the neighborhood-offsets refactor instead, which targeted the worst-McCabe function and yielded the largest measured win.
